comment_text.sn22.79:1.2: Compare the stock description of such recollection: “There, I was named this, my clan was that, I looked like this, and that was my food. This was how I felt pleasure and pain, and that was how my life ended” (eg. SN 16.9:13.1). Also see the different ways the self is conceived as being reborn in states described as “percipient”, “formed”, and so on in DN 1.
comment_text.sn22.79:2.5: For the other aggregates, the noun is illustrated with the verb, but rūpa has no verbal form. Instead, the text puns with the unrelated verb ruppati. Ruppati would be better translated as “inflict”, but I use “deform” to maintain the play on words. The actual etymology of rūpa is unclear (see note on SN 12.2:11.5).
comment_text.sn22.79:4.4: “Color” is a more sophisticated and less universal aspect of awareness than “feeling”, reflecting the fact that “perception” relates to higher-order functions like recognition and interpretation, which are involved in concept formation.
comment_text.sn22.79:5.2: The text uses three forms of the same word. A literal rendering might be: “They activate the activated; that’s why they’re called activators.”
comment_text.sn22.79:5.4: Literally, “they make conditioned form into the state of being form”.
comment_text.sn22.79:5.5: The aggregates in this life were generated by the kammic choices in past lives, and continue to be shaped by choices in this life. Unless kammically potent choices are relinquished, they will go on to generate future lives as well.
comment_text.sn22.79:6.4: It seems that taste was a common way of illustrating consciousness. Aitareya Upaniṣad 3.1 describes the Self as that by which one sees, hears, smells, tastes, speaks, and “cognizes sweet and sour” (svādu cāsvādu ca vijānāti). Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 3.2.4 says “one cognizes tastes with the tongue”. The explanation at MN 43:4.5 differs, as there it is talking about meditation, not rebirth.
comment_text.sn22.79:7.2: The verb “bite” is used as a vivid image for the way the aggregates constantly afflict or “itch”. Compare the Vedic image of Indra as the “dragon-biter” (Rig Veda 3.45.2). Here it can also be seen as continuing the idea of consciousness as the taster.
comment_text.sn22.79:12.3: The verbs visineti and ussineti, unique to this passage, are from the root si, “bind”.
comment_text.sn22.79:14.1: “Remain” (ṭhita) means “live on”. This passage concerns the arahant before his final passing. This passage recalls the one who “neither picks up nor puts down” in the Aṭṭhakavagga (eg. Snp 4.3:8.3).
comment_text.sn22.79:15.4: Elsewhere, this verse expresses the mystic wonder of these deities at the depth of an arahant’s meditation and that upon which it is based (AN 11.9, Thag 18.1:34.4). Here it seems less pertinent, as there has been no mention of meditation and its basis.

root_text.sn22.79:1.2: “Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā anekavihitaṁ pubbenivāsaṁ anussaramānā anussaranti sabbete pañcupādānakkhandhe anussaranti etesaṁ vā aññataraṁ.
root_text.sn22.79:1.5: iti vā hi, bhikkhave, anussaramāno rūpaṁyeva anussarati.
root_text.sn22.79:1.7: iti vā hi, bhikkhave, anussaramāno vedanaṁyeva anussarati.
root_text.sn22.79:1.11: iti vā hi, bhikkhave, anussaramāno viññāṇameva anussarati.
root_text.sn22.79:2.4: Sītenapi ruppati, uṇhenapi ruppati, jighacchāyapi ruppati, pipāsāyapi ruppati, ḍaṁsamakasavātātapasarīsapasamphassenapi ruppati.
root_text.sn22.79:3.4: Sukhampi vedayati, dukkhampi vedayati, adukkhamasukhampi vedayati.
root_text.sn22.79:4.4: Nīlampi sañjānāti, pītakampi sañjānāti, lohitakampi sañjānāti, odātampi sañjānāti.
root_text.sn22.79:5.4: Rūpaṁ rūpattāya saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharonti, vedanaṁ vedanattāya saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharonti, saññaṁ saññattāya saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharonti, saṅkhāre saṅkhārattāya saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharonti, viññāṇaṁ viññāṇattāya saṅkhatamabhisaṅkharonti.
root_text.sn22.79:6.4: Ambilampi vijānāti, tittakampi vijānāti, kaṭukampi vijānāti, madhurampi vijānāti, khārikampi vijānāti, akhārikampi vijānāti, loṇikampi vijānāti, aloṇikampi vijānāti.
root_text.sn22.79:7.1: Tatra, bhikkhave, sutavā ariyasāvako iti paṭisañcikkhati:
root_text.sn22.79:7.3: Atītampāhaṁ addhānaṁ evameva rūpena khajjiṁ, seyyathāpi etarahi paccuppannena rūpena khajjāmi.
root_text.sn22.79:7.4: Ahañceva kho pana anāgataṁ rūpaṁ abhinandeyyaṁ, anāgatampāhaṁ addhānaṁ evameva rūpena khajjeyyaṁ, seyyathāpi etarahi paccuppannena rūpena khajjāmī’ti.
root_text.sn22.79:7.5: So iti paṭisaṅkhāya atītasmiṁ rūpasmiṁ anapekkho hoti;
root_text.sn22.79:7.7: paccuppannassa rūpassa nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipanno hoti.
root_text.sn22.79:8.2: Atītampāhaṁ addhānaṁ evameva vedanāya khajjiṁ, seyyathāpi etarahi paccuppannāya vedanāya khajjāmi.
root_text.sn22.79:8.3: Ahañceva kho pana anāgataṁ vedanaṁ abhinandeyyaṁ;
root_text.sn22.79:8.4: anāgatampāhaṁ addhānaṁ evameva vedanāya khajjeyyaṁ, seyyathāpi etarahi paccuppannāya vedanāya khajjāmī’ti.
root_text.sn22.79:8.5: So iti paṭisaṅkhāya atītāya vedanāya anapekkho hoti;
root_text.sn22.79:8.7: paccuppannāya vedanāya nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipanno hoti.
root_text.sn22.79:9.3: Atītampāhaṁ addhānaṁ evameva saṅkhārehi khajjiṁ, seyyathāpi etarahi paccuppannehi saṅkhārehi khajjāmīti.
root_text.sn22.79:9.4: Ahañceva kho pana anāgate saṅkhāre abhinandeyyaṁ;
root_text.sn22.79:9.5: anāgatampāhaṁ addhānaṁ evameva saṅkhārehi khajjeyyaṁ, seyyathāpi etarahi paccuppannehi saṅkhārehi khajjāmī’ti.
root_text.sn22.79:9.6: So iti paṭisaṅkhāya atītesu saṅkhāresu anapekkho hoti;
root_text.sn22.79:9.8: paccuppannānaṁ saṅkhārānaṁ nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipanno hoti.
root_text.sn22.79:10.2: Atītampi addhānaṁ evameva viññāṇena khajjiṁ, seyyathāpi etarahi paccuppannena viññāṇena khajjāmi.
root_text.sn22.79:10.3: Ahañceva kho pana anāgataṁ viññāṇaṁ abhinandeyyaṁ;
root_text.sn22.79:10.4: anāgatampāhaṁ addhānaṁ evameva viññāṇena khajjeyyaṁ, seyyathāpi etarahi paccuppannena viññāṇena khajjāmī’ti.
root_text.sn22.79:10.5: So iti paṭisaṅkhāya atītasmiṁ viññāṇasmiṁ anapekkho hoti;
root_text.sn22.79:10.7: paccuppannassa viññāṇassa nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipanno hoti.
root_text.sn22.79:11.4: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vā taṁ sukhaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn22.79:11.6: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vipariṇāmadhammaṁ, kallaṁ nu taṁ samanupassituṁ:
root_text.sn22.79:11.14: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vā taṁ sukhaṁ vā”ti?
root_text.sn22.79:11.16: “Yaṁ panāniccaṁ dukkhaṁ vipariṇāmadhammaṁ, kallaṁ nu taṁ samanupassituṁ:
root_text.sn22.79:11.19: “Tasmātiha, bhikkhave, yaṁ kiñci rūpaṁ atītānāgatapaccuppannaṁ ajjhattaṁ vā bahiddhā vā oḷārikaṁ vā sukhumaṁ vā hīnaṁ vā paṇītaṁ vā yaṁ dūre santike vā, sabbaṁ rūpaṁ: ‘netaṁ mama, nesohamasmi, na meso attā’ti evametaṁ yathābhūtaṁ sammappaññāya daṭṭhabbaṁ.
root_text.sn22.79:11.23: yaṁ kiñci viññāṇaṁ atītānāgatapaccuppannaṁ …pe… yaṁ dūre santike vā, sabbaṁ viññāṇaṁ: ‘netaṁ mama, nesohamasmi, na meso attā’ti evametaṁ yathābhūtaṁ sammappaññāya daṭṭhabbaṁ.
root_text.sn22.79:12.1: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, ariyasāvako apacināti, no ācināti;
root_text.sn22.79:13.1: Evaṁ passaṁ, bhikkhave, sutavā ariyasāvako rūpasmimpi nibbindati, vedanāyapi … saññāyapi … saṅkhāresupi … viññāṇasmimpi nibbindati.
root_text.sn22.79:13.2: Nibbindaṁ virajjati; virāgā vimuccati. Vimuttasmiṁ vimuttamiti ñāṇaṁ hoti.
root_text.sn22.79:13.3: ‘Khīṇā jāti, vusitaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, kataṁ karaṇīyaṁ, nāparaṁ itthattāyā’ti pajānāti.
root_text.sn22.79:14.1: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu nevācināti na apacināti, apacinitvā ṭhito; neva pajahati na upādiyati, pajahitvā ṭhito; neva visineti na ussineti, visinetvā ṭhito; neva vidhūpeti na sandhūpeti vidhūpetvā ṭhito
root_text.sn22.79:14.2: Kiñca nevācināti na apacināti, apacinitvā ṭhito?
root_text.sn22.79:14.3: Rūpaṁ nevācināti na apacināti, apacinitvā ṭhito;
root_text.sn22.79:14.7: viññāṇaṁ nevācināti na apacināti, apacinitvā ṭhito.
root_text.sn22.79:14.8: Kiñca neva pajahati na upādiyati, pajahitvā ṭhito?
root_text.sn22.79:14.9: Rūpaṁ neva pajahati na upādiyati, pajahitvā ṭhito;
root_text.sn22.79:14.13: viññāṇaṁ neva pajahati na upādiyati, pajahitvā ṭhito.
root_text.sn22.79:14.14: Kiñca neva visineti na ussineti, visinetvā ṭhito?
root_text.sn22.79:14.15: Rūpaṁ neva visineti na ussineti, visinetvā ṭhito;
root_text.sn22.79:14.19: viññāṇaṁ neva visineti na ussineti, visinetvā ṭhito.
root_text.sn22.79:14.20: Kiñca neva vidhūpeti na sandhūpeti, vidhūpetvā ṭhito?
root_text.sn22.79:14.21: Rūpaṁ neva vidhūpeti na sandhūpeti, vidhūpetvā ṭhito;
root_text.sn22.79:14.25: viññāṇaṁ neva vidhūpeti na sandhūpeti, vidhūpetvā ṭhito.
root_text.sn22.79:14.26: Evaṁvimuttacittaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhuṁ saindā devā sabrahmakā sapajāpatikā ārakāva namassanti:

translation_text.sn22.79:1.2: “Mendicants, whatever ascetics and brahmins recollect many kinds of past lives, all recollect the five grasping aggregates, or one of them.
translation_text.sn22.79:1.4: ‘I had such form in the past.’
translation_text.sn22.79:1.5: Recollecting thus, it’s only form that they recollect.
translation_text.sn22.79:1.11: Recollecting thus, it’s only consciousness that they recollect.
translation_text.sn22.79:2.1: And why do you call it form?
translation_text.sn22.79:2.2: It’s deformed; that’s why it’s called ‘form’.
translation_text.sn22.79:2.4: Deformed by cold, heat, hunger, and thirst, and deformed by the touch of flies, mosquitoes, wind, sun, and reptiles.
translation_text.sn22.79:2.5: It’s deformed; that’s why it’s called ‘form’.
translation_text.sn22.79:3.1: And why do you call it feeling?
translation_text.sn22.79:3.2: It feels; that’s why it’s called ‘feeling’.
translation_text.sn22.79:3.4: It feels pleasure, pain, and neutral.
translation_text.sn22.79:3.5: It feels; that’s why it’s called ‘feeling’.
translation_text.sn22.79:4.1: And why do you call it perception?
translation_text.sn22.79:4.2: It perceives; that’s why it’s called ‘perception’.
translation_text.sn22.79:4.4: It perceives blue, yellow, red, and white.
translation_text.sn22.79:4.5: It perceives; that’s why it’s called ‘perception’.
translation_text.sn22.79:5.1: And why do you call them choices?
translation_text.sn22.79:5.2: Choices produce conditioned phenomena; that’s why they’re called ‘choices’.
translation_text.sn22.79:5.3: And what are the conditioned phenomena that they produce?
translation_text.sn22.79:5.4: Form is a conditioned phenomenon; choices are what make it into form. Feeling is a conditioned phenomenon; choices are what make it into feeling. Perception is a conditioned phenomenon; choices are what make it into perception. Choices are conditioned phenomena; choices are what make them into choices. Consciousness is a conditioned phenomenon; choices are what make it into consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.79:5.5: Choices produce conditioned phenomena; that’s why they’re called ‘choices’.
translation_text.sn22.79:6.1: And why do you call it consciousness?
translation_text.sn22.79:6.2: It cognizes; that’s why it’s called ‘consciousness’.
translation_text.sn22.79:6.4: It cognizes sour, bitter, pungent, sweet, hot, mild, salty, and bland.
translation_text.sn22.79:6.5: It cognizes; that’s why it’s called ‘consciousness’.
translation_text.sn22.79:7.1: A learned noble disciple reflects on this:
translation_text.sn22.79:7.3: In the past I was also bitten by form just like now.
translation_text.sn22.79:7.4: If I were to look forward to enjoying form in the future, I’d be bitten by form in the future just as I am today.’
translation_text.sn22.79:7.5: Reflecting like this they are not concerned with past form,
translation_text.sn22.79:7.6: they don’t look forward to enjoying future form,
translation_text.sn22.79:7.7: and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present form.
translation_text.sn22.79:10.2: In the past I was also bitten by consciousness just like now.
translation_text.sn22.79:10.3: If I were to look forward to enjoying consciousness in the future, I’d be bitten by consciousness in the future just as I am today.’
translation_text.sn22.79:10.5: Reflecting like this they are not concerned with past consciousness,
translation_text.sn22.79:10.6: they don’t look forward to enjoying future consciousness,
translation_text.sn22.79:10.7: and they practice for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding present consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.79:11.4: “But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?”
translation_text.sn22.79:11.6: “But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus:
translation_text.sn22.79:11.7: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
translation_text.sn22.79:11.14: “But if it’s impermanent, is it suffering or happiness?”
translation_text.sn22.79:11.16: “But if it’s impermanent, suffering, and perishable, is it fit to be regarded thus:
translation_text.sn22.79:11.17: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’?”
translation_text.sn22.79:11.19: “So you should truly see any kind of form at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: all form—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
translation_text.sn22.79:11.20: You should truly see any kind of feeling …
translation_text.sn22.79:11.23: consciousness at all—past, future, or present; internal or external; solid or subtle; inferior or superior; far or near: all consciousness—with right understanding: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
translation_text.sn22.79:12.1: This is called a noble disciple who gets rid of and doesn’t accumulate;
translation_text.sn22.79:12.2: who gives up and doesn’t grasp;
translation_text.sn22.79:12.4: who dissipates and doesn’t get clouded.
translation_text.sn22.79:12.5: And what things do they get rid of and not accumulate?
translation_text.sn22.79:12.6: They get rid of form and don’t accumulate it.
translation_text.sn22.79:12.11: And what things do they give up and not grasp?
translation_text.sn22.79:12.12: They give up form and don’t grasp it.
translation_text.sn22.79:12.17: And what things do they unmesh and not enmesh?
translation_text.sn22.79:12.18: They unmesh form and don’t enmesh it.
translation_text.sn22.79:12.23: And what things do they dissipate and not get clouded by?
translation_text.sn22.79:12.24: They dissipate form and don’t get clouded by it.
translation_text.sn22.79:12.28: consciousness and don’t get clouded by it.
translation_text.sn22.79:13.1: Seeing this, a learned noble disciple grows disillusioned with form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.79:13.2: Being disillusioned, desire fades away. When desire fades away they’re freed. When they’re freed, they know they’re freed.
translation_text.sn22.79:13.3: They understand: ‘Rebirth is ended, the spiritual journey has been completed, what had to be done has been done, there is nothing further for this place.’
translation_text.sn22.79:14.1: This is called a mendicant who neither gets rid of nor accumulates, but remains after getting rid. They neither give up nor grasp, but remain after giving up. They neither unmesh nor enmesh, but remain after unmeshing. They neither dissipate nor get clouded, but remain after dissipating.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.2: And what things do they neither get rid of nor accumulate, but remain after getting rid of them?
translation_text.sn22.79:14.3: They neither get rid of nor accumulate form, but remain after getting rid of it.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.4: They neither get rid of nor accumulate feeling …
translation_text.sn22.79:14.7: consciousness, but remain after getting rid of it.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.8: And what things do they neither give up nor grasp, but remain after giving them up?
translation_text.sn22.79:14.9: They neither give up nor grasp form, but remain after giving it up.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.10: They neither give up nor grasp feeling …
translation_text.sn22.79:14.13: consciousness, but remain after giving it up.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.14: And what things do they neither discard nor amass, but remain after discarding them?
translation_text.sn22.79:14.15: They neither unmesh nor enmesh form, but remain after unmeshing it.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.16: They neither unmesh nor enmesh feeling …
translation_text.sn22.79:14.19: consciousness, but remain after unmeshing it.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.20: And what things do they neither dissipate nor get clouded by, but remain after dissipating them?
translation_text.sn22.79:14.21: They neither dissipate nor get clouded by form, but remain after dissipating it.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.22: They neither dissipate nor get clouded by feeling …
translation_text.sn22.79:14.25: consciousness, but remain after dissipating it.
translation_text.sn22.79:14.26: When a mendicant’s mind is freed like this, the gods together with Indra, the Divinity, and the Progenitor worship them from afar:
translation_text.sn22.79:15.2: Homage to you, supreme among men!
